Our first-time guest here on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, he shared powerful insights into virtually every subsector of the group and every major trend driving supply, demand, and pricing. And that includes things like the AI boom and the ways we’re powering it as a country, something that we will be talking about long after the guns go silent in the Middle East.

He is Ken Medlock, Senior Director at the Center for Energy Studies, at The Baker Institute for Public Policy, which is located at Rice University. Ken, thank you so much for taking some time out here to chat. I know, I haven’t had you on the podcast before, so, for somebody who is not familiar with your background, why don’t you just take a minute or two to talk about what your history is in the energy industry and what you do there at The Baker Institute?

Ken Medlock: Yeah, sure. So, it’s a pleasure to be here. I currently lead the Center for Energy Studies here at The Baker Institute. We have been ranked the number one think tank on energy and natural resources in the world. Really pride ourselves in being data-centered, grounded in foundational theories, economics, engineering, political science, all of the above, have a very diverse background of fellows and staff working on a variety of issues ranging from oil and gas all the way to sustainability and circularity.

So, we run the gamut. And that's largely because what we try to do is elevate conversations. We do not advocate, but we definitely try to elevate discussions so that rational policy and good decisions can be made.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, I appreciate that background. And in a market like this, an environment like this, certainly trying to keep a level head can be a little difficult. So, with that being said, let's talk about the energy markets here from 30,000 feet up. We all know what's going on in the Middle East and how the conflict is driving the major energy markets. How would you set the stage and describe the state of affairs right now in energy?

Ken Medlock: It's a time of tremendous uncertainty and turmoil, without a doubt. But I have to caution everybody because there's no such thing as normal in energy commodity markets. There's always something that is throwing a pebble in the pond, if you will, and creating ripples. Arguably what's going on right now in the Middle East is throwing a boulder in the pond. So, it's creating a lot of consternation, generating a lot of uncertainty and the impacts are not being felt symmetrically.

So, in the United States, for example, because we are a net energy exporter, because we've got an abundance of all forms of energy really, we're not immune from the price increases that we're seeing globally, particularly in the crude oil petroleum product space, but we don't have to worry about running out, which is a little bit different. On the natural gas front, we're not seeing the same price impacts that you're seeing in Europe and Asia, largely because we're an exporter and we are bound up in terms of how much capacity we have to export. So, even though there is an increased appetite for U.S. LNG because the Qatari gas that normally would flow out into the global market is not at the moment. We can't flow anymore. And so, you actually haven't seen domestic natural gas prices rise. So, there's nuance as well in this time of tremendous uncertainty.

But I'd say the one thing that is probably underappreciated historically in energy but is starting to rise to the top is really how interconnected energy supply chains are. So, there's been a lot of discussion about what's going on in the Middle East right now could trigger a landslide towards electrification and diversification from oil and gas, but there is a word of caution about that. In particular, people don't always internalize how deeply intertwined the supply chains, even for electrification, are with oil and gas.

I say that because about 30% of the world's helium comes from the Middle East. That is currently not moving, which means it's not just about the balloons we blow up at parties for our kids, right? It's also an industrial gas and helium is a gas that really doesn't have a substitute in semiconductor fabrication. So, if you're going to make semiconductors, which are critical for all of the control mechanisms for electric power systems, wind farms, solar farms, this move to electrify, you need helium in order to facilitate semiconductor fabrication and production and movement into that space.

And right now, there's concerns about, and I think there might have actually been one or two issued already, but force majeures related to semiconductor plants in Asia who simply do not have the helium they need to produce what they normally would produce. So, understanding how deeply intertwined these supply chains is vital because helium comes from producing natural gas.

When you think about sulfur and ammonia, right, you're talking about things that are connected to natural gas and petroleum refining, and that feeds into the agricultural sector. And you need those things for nitrogenous-based fertilizers and other sulfur-based inputs in the agricultural sector to increase crop yield, maintain healthy resilient systems. And the disruption that we're feeling has ramifications around the world across multiple things. And so, it's not just an oil and gas story, but it really does signal how deeply intertwined all of these supply chains are.

Mike Larson: Yeah. And how would you compare that to the environment that we had in past crises, Gulf War I, Gulf War II, or even as far back as the Arab Oil Embargo? It's a different world, I guess it's fair to say, correct?

Ken Medlock: It is a very different world. In fact, when we think about the Arab Oil Embargo, you're talking about a global oil market that was less than half the size of what it is today, right? So, not quite as large, significant disruption that triggered a massive shift on the policy front. One of the most lasting and probably substantial shifts on policy was an aim to diversify supply chain.

So, one of the things we can do to mitigate the cost of disruptions is to increase arbitrage, the different ways we can trade, right? And storage, so the creation of strategic stocks is a very robust way to increase arbitrage opportunity. That's why the SPR was born. It's why the International Energy Agency was born and its strategic stocks. It's a way to mitigate or create a buffer for these disruptions, but the global oil markets also expanded radically and there's a lot more sources of supply than there were back then. So, the ability to flex is a little bit greater, but the size of this disruption is unprecedented just in terms of the amount of oil that is being held off the market.

But what that's going to do is trigger a significant interest in diversifying pathways out of the region, but also it's going to signal a lot of interest in diversifying energy systems more generally in ways that are feasible, right? I mean, reality is a binding constraint. So, the fact that these supply chains are all connected, cost matters, affordability matters, it's not just about oil and gas, it's about everything, how it all connects, and diversification will be the name of the game for energy supply chains going forward.

I will point out that the price today that we're living through in the Brent world, if you will, so that's the marker in Europe, right? It's still not quite at the levels we saw in the double spike on the heels of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. And it's also not at the levels we saw for five consecutive years from 2010 through 2015, where the price averaged north of $100 a barrel every single year.

So, while this is a shock definitely, particularly on the amount of barrels that are being held off the market, the price environment in terms of the short-run implications is still not quite what we saw in just the last 15 years. So, to make claims that this is going to have dramatic implications for demand destruction, which is long-term capital decay, if you will, in replacement, I think that's a little bit outrageous at this point.

Mike Larson: Okay. Let's try and talk a little bit about numbers here. I mean, how many barrels a day are we essentially short as a result of what's happening? How much coverage can you get from the SPR here in the U.S. and from some of these other global stockpiles? How long does it last, I guess, to cover that gap or at least some of it?

Ken Medlock: So, I'll start with storage. So, IEA member countries, if they import a required to by agreement hold at a minimum of 90 days of their imported supply. So, what that tells you is that every IEA country, and they're all in compliance if they're importing, has the ability to offset at least ninety days, most a little bit more. And so, there is a buffer there that will allow for the storm to be weathered. The critical thing there is, when do you release, right? Because you don't want to trigger a release every time the price spikes. You want to do it when things really become an emergency because the thing about strategic stocks, if you use them today, you don't have them tomorrow. So, you have to make sure that you use them in the most efficient manner possible.

If you're an exporting country, you're not required to hold strategic stock. So, the fact that the U.S. actually does hold an SPR is interesting because in point of fact, as a member of the IEA, it's not required to, but it does. The max send out from the SPR is rated at about 4.4 or so million barrels a day, so quite substantial. It has never quite reached that level. So, max rating and what is actually ultimately achievable are open questions, right, in terms of whether or not we could actually hit that mark. But the amount that you'll see released from the SPR and from IEA strategic stocks, that's usually done in a coordinated way so that you don't flood the market and you actually use those stocks in as judicious a manner as possible.

Now, in terms of barrels disrupted, on a daily basis on average, and this will fluctuate day-to-day obviously, but on average last year it was around 17, 17.5 million barrels of oil and petroleum products daily that flowed through the Strait of Hormuz. And that has been cut off for the most part. Now, there are some shipments that are moving through in agreement with the Iranian government at the moment, but it's a small amount.

Saudi Arabia has redirected flows across the Arabian Peninsula into the Red Sea via the East-West pipeline, and that was constructed in the wake of the overthrow of the Shah of Iran in the late 1970s and was commissioned in the early 1980s. Rated capacity is 5 million barrels a day, but you can flush the NGL lines that run parallel and add that up another 2 million barrels or so. So, from what I understand, they've actually done that, and they're moving 7 million barrels a day across and loading at the Port of Yanbu in the Red Sea.

So, what that basically means is that you still have disrupted over half of that 17.5 million barrels a day of daily flow. So, you'd need to be able to make up roughly 10 million barrels a day if you were going to offset it precisely from what you're going to use from strategic stocks. But in reality, you're not going to see that amount released because there are demand-side mitigation protocols also taking place.

So, you've seen cuts in refinery runs, particularly in Asia, where a lot of those barrels from the Middle East ultimately land. And so that rationing behavior effectively reduces demand so that you don't need the full amount. And so, on net, you're probably looking at, given the combined rationing, 7 million to 8 million barrels a day that were short. Strategic stocks can make up that delta, but only for a short period of time. And so, the market really has to look to other mechanisms to balance.

And one of the things that you're seeing right now is, we came into this year with a bit of a supply overhang. And so, the market was very robustly supplied. There were some agencies predicting a net surplus of supply of 3 million to 3.5 million barrels a day this year. So, if that indeed was going to be the case, now you're only talking about a roughly 4 million barrel a day gap to fill. Right? So, you could see where the numbers whittle down pretty quickly, but it's still sizable. It's still definitely sizable.

Mike Larson: Yeah. Let me ask you this. I mean, there's a couple of day lag between as we're recording this versus when it'll air. So, as we're talking, Brent's around 108, give or take. What level do you start talking? What price levels do you start talking about, of serious demand destruction possibility or potential?

Ken Medlock: So, that's a great question, and you're right. Tomorrow could bring a completely different environment. There could be a tweet on Truth Social that drops the price by $5 and could go the other way, right? But demand destruction, and I'm an economist, so I speak about it a little differently, right? Demand destruction is not about walking up a demand curve and allowing price elasticity to erode demand because price goes higher.

Demand destruction is about price gets to a level where you're never going to want to use that commodity again and you start to retire capital stocks. So that you truly destroy the basis for demand to ever return to where it was. So, this would be like price rising and the price of the pump – of crude oil rising, then the price at the pump rising, and then consumers in mass going out and trading in their motor vehicles, either for more higher efficiency vehicles or electric vehicles, right?

We saw a plus $100 a barrel range for, like I said earlier, for five years straight. Yet over that window, demand globally grew by 7 million barrels a day. So, we did not see demand destruction in that instance. In this instance, I think there's a little bit different concern because of the actual shortages, physical shortages, that you're seeing in Asia. But I would argue that if you start to see prices come down by the time we get to this summer, by the time we get to the end of this year, a lot of this will be a bad memory because the capital that's in place will not be retired. It will go right back to work.

Now, there are going to be steps taken to provide insurance against future disruptions, right? And that will take multiple forms, including other pathways out of the Middle East region, like pipeline routes, etcetera. But it's also going to take the form of increases in efficiency, ways to think about balancing markets differently by alternative sources of supply. So, you might see an interest in increased production and – exploration and production, I should say, in West Africa, in Latin America where there's some pretty robust opportunities available. So, it's going to be a portfolio approach to dealing with this, like every disruption ultimately lends itself to, but insurance will be the name of the game no matter how long this lasts coming out the back end.

Mike Larson: Got it. You were at the CERA conference recently, so obviously had a lot of conversations with people active in the industry in one form or another. Can you maybe sum up how those conversations went? What are they saying about these developments and how they see the energy market unfolding in the coming month?

Ken Medlock: So, the conversations were interesting, because as a conference planner, I felt for S&P Global because you're putting speaker rosters together, you're developing what the agenda is going to look like. And then, of course, we have Venezuela happened in January, and then this happened on the last day of February, right, with Iran. Talk about shocks to the system. So, they had to flex pretty rapidly to try to bring those discussions into focus. Even where there wasn't tremendous flex because there were programmatic elements that were about other issues, Iran and Venezuela crept in, right?

So, it's the topic du jour, if you will, and rightfully so because it is a major source of uncertainty across the energy landscape. The attitudes varied. In general, I'd say negative, but there were some glimmers of hope that were shared by different conference participants, but I think at the end of the day, there's just so much uncertainty it was hard for people to really digest and speak authoritatively about anything.

And so, you found a lot of leaning back into what research have I done in the past? How does this look like or feel like something that's happened in the past? Even though it is starkly different in many ways just because of the physical barrel, that disruption is larger than we've ever seen. So, uncertainty ruled the discussion, if you will, but you could see the wheel spinning. There's a lot of work going on behind the scenes to figure out how to deal with this disruption.

Mike Larson: Well, obviously, the war and its impact on the markets has to be the biggest point of this conversation, but I'd be remiss to, if I didn't come up or bring up a few other topics in energy that would be of greater interest if, for some reason, we were able to get a peace deal tomorrow. And that would be things like the impact of AI-driven demand and power on U.S. resources, natural gas in particular. Any thoughts on, again, if the war eventually moves to the back pages, how are some of those developments going to impact the energy industry here in the U.S.?

Ken Medlock: That's also a great question. In fact, there's been a lot of discussion about how the war is impacting data center build-out because there's concern about natural gas prices and how that feeds into electricity prices and what will data centers do, particularly those that have already made decisions to site co-located natural gas generation at these massive facilities. I mean, you're talking about one facility up in Ohio, I believe it is, that's planning to build something like 9 gigawatts of power generation. There's one in Louisiana that's on the 5 gigawatt, and it's all gas generation, right? Mind you. So, this is a lot of load.

The biggest impediment for the United States is going to be infrastructure development. Can we build the pipelines to move gas from where it's cheap to where it's not? Right? So, over the last roughly month, I believe, the price at Waha, which is the hub price in West Texas, has not traded in positive territory, which tells you there's just way too much gas there that can't move to find a home. So, it really is about building infrastructure so that you can move that gas from where it's not needed to where it is so that you can reduce the price impacts of expanding infrastructure behind those constraints.

But in the power space, you're seeing data centers really go to more co-located generation and less grid-connected generation for two reasons. It does help overcome concerns about spiking electricity prices in the locality where these are sited, right? Because they're generating their own power. But it also speeds up the time to which they can develop because if you're co-locating generation and you're not grid connected, you don't have to get in this massively long interconnect queue where studies have to be done to assess the impact on the grid of a large load being located in a particular place on the grid, right?

So, data centers are thinking about how to move more quickly because the demand for what they provide is not going down. It's going up. It's increasingly going up. And interestingly, and I haven't seen any data on this, but connecting back to the conflict in Iran, I've seen a lot of AI-generated graphics lately on what's going on, where resources are located, how they're sourced, where they end up landing. So, I would hazard a guess that the demands on AI are actually going up as a result of this event, not going down.

So, there's without a doubt a need for this stuff, but local acceptance is the other big issue. You're seeing in some places around the United States a pushback, right? And that manifests ultimately in a regulatory pushback, not just a pushback by local residents. And in other places, you're not seeing that pushback. And so, I'm going to – I think what that's likely going to lead to is clusters forming, large industrial clusters forming where you'll see data centers co-located with access to energy infrastructure. So, this can be wires, generation, natural gas pipelines, co-located near nuclear power facilities. We've seen some elements of that rising as well.

Access to water, plenty of land, and communities that are accepting of siting there. Because typically when you have these industrial clusters form, it does change basically the tax reality in the region. And you start to see schools getting refurbished and new stadiums being built and all things that the local community likes. So, where you see pushback, you're also going to see acceptance in other places, and likely that's going to trigger clusters forming. So, it's going to be interesting to watch this unfold.

Mike Larson: Got it. The tail end of our conversation here, and since you mentioned it, I was going to ask it anyway, and that would be the outlook for nuclear power. How much of a solution is that for not just the AI-driven demand, but the U.S. power industry in general? Some quick thoughts on that, how you see that evolving?

Ken Medlock: Yeah. Nuclear power is intriguing. There's really no way if you want to put your long run hat and think about 25, 30, 40 years from now. There's no way to really think about a fully decarbonized power system without nuclear power because it provides baseload controllable generation that is necessary for any grid. The one thing for reliability that people sometimes forget and any system operator will tell you this or any marketer in the power generation space will tell you this, it's about controllability. And nuclear provides you a base that you can build on that is very controllable, very reliable. Once it's on, it's on. So, when you think about the long run, it has a massive role to play.

Now, how that manifests is anybody's guess. It's probably not going to be large-scale utility power that is incredibly expensive, often runs into local opposition, doesn't really have the same economic prowess that a lot of other smaller, lighter, less capital-intensive generation sources have. But this is where advanced nuclear technologies come into play, where you get into small modular and micronuclear technologies, and we know how to do it. That's not the issue. The technology is not the issue. In fact, you see a lot of this stuff deployed on the micro-level scale in ship fleets, particularly in the U.S. Navy all over the world.

So, we know how to do it. It's really about how do you generate enough scale in the commercial landscape to drive costs down to make it economically viable. And the U.S. government has taken some steps in that direction by announcing a project whereby they're ordering from private producers, micronuclear reactors for deployment at forward operating bases all over the world. So, will that be enough to kick-start this order process so you get scale and drive down cost? I think that remains to be seen, but without a doubt, it's a potentially very positive input into not just data centers, right, and powering those, but just the future of the grid more generally.

I'd be remiss not to also mention advanced geothermal because that's one that utilizes known technologies that were developed in the oil and gas space. So, deep understanding of the subsurface, how fractures actually communicate, how you actually flow water and subsurface features so you can generate steam. I mean, all this is part of the advanced geothermal world, and there's a whole lot of expertise that was developed in that space over the last two decades that's finding its way from the oil and gas arena into the advanced geothermal space. So, that's another one to watch as well.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, Ken, you're going to be joining us for the MoneyShow Masters Symposium in Dallas, May 14th to 16th. Somebody watching this might be thinking about coming to that event. Can you just briefly preview what they're going to get out of your presentation, with, of course, the obvious caveat that things will change between now and then?

Ken Medlock: I was about to say, that's what we agreed when I agreed to come talk. I was like, let's leave it open so we can flex because the world is changing rapidly. But without a doubt, there will be some connectivity to the reality on the ground at that current moment in time to deeper fundamentals so we can understand how things might evolve going forward.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, Ken, I really do want to appreciate you or say thank you for taking some time out to chat. Viewers, if you're watching this or if you're listening to the audio version, Ken will be speaking at that event. A lot of ground to cover in Dallas in May, so be sure to check out the schedule and come see us there. Ken, thank you so much.

Ken Medlock: Absolutely. It's been a pleasure.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HEICO: Iran War Adds Pressure, But Premium Compression Creates A 'Buy' Opportunity
data.description:
HEICO Corporation (HEI), (HEI.A) shares have lost nearly 20% since my last report, substantially underperforming the S&P 500’s 5% loss. The broader aerospace and defense industry has also not fared well on the war developments in Iran, and as a leading MRO company and supplier, it is no surprise that the stock came under pressure. In this report, I provide a contrarian angle, as I believe that while potentially the pressures are real, there also are opportunities and partial shielding that the market seems to ignore.

HEICO Underperformed Substantially Against Aerospace And Defense Peers

Looking at the return levels, we see that since my last report, HEICO shares lost 18.7% of their value, while the S&P 500 lost 4.6%, and the aerospace and defense ETF ITA lost 5.3%. So, it is clear that the company has underperformed substantially, even compared to the peer group.

Why The Share Price Is Confusing

The rationale for the share price decline is seemingly relatively simple. HEICO is a leading supplier and MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) company to the aerospace and defense industry, and with high oil prices, airlines are inclined to cut flights, and economic growth supporting traffic growth may soften. Flights being cut is definitely not a hyperbolic statement; it is already happening as United Airlines cut some flights due to high oil prices. Furthermore, we note that HEICO has been valued at a premium to peers for a long time. The company has a median trading multiple of 37.5x EV/EBITDA at a time when peers traded at 23x EV/EBITDA. That suggests a 63% overvaluation. So, the share price decline looks a lot like a company being pulled back to reality.

However, that viewpoint would be a stark oversimplification. One major reason for HEICO to trade at a premium to peers is that the company derives its growth through a combination of organic and inorganic growth opportunities. It is almost as if, by design, the company’s EV/EBITDA has been elevated compared to peers because it engages in acquisitions where the company buys other suppliers and MRO providers and brings the margin profiles of those companies to the HEICO standard. Oftentimes, the acquisitions are financed with debt, leading to a higher EV/EBITDA, and next to that, investors have also assigned a premium because HEICO excels in the practice of acquiring companies and integrating them.

We also note that the broader sell-off may result in HEICO being able to acquire companies at more attractive prices. During the fourth quarter earnings call, Carlos Macau (CFO of HEICO) provided a very insightful comment on the leverage and acquisitions:

For the right transaction, for the right deal for our shareholders, we would take on additional leverage. I don't think that our company, the culture, and the way we do business would suggest we'd like to have permanent leverage in the 5 or 6 range. But if we had to spike up the 4, 5, 6x to do a deal, and I felt comfortable that within 12 to 18, 24 months, you could get that leverage back down to a manageable number, we would certainly do that if it was good for our shareholders.

I think as a sort of status quo, we like leverage to be somewhere around 2x. That's a comfortable spot for us. Right now, we're -- our permanent debt, if you would, or our bonds are sitting at about 1 turn of EBITDA. That's very comfortable. I think we could probably carry 2 turns of debt in a permanent sort of financing posture and be very comfortable with HEICO with the cash generation that we have.

So, there is a positive angle here: with the broader sell-off, HEICO could acquire companies at a lower price. In other words, the current softness for aerospace and defense valuations opens up the opportunity for HEICO to acquire companies with a better return on invested capital.

From a financial perspective, there is also the concern regarding demand. If planes fly less, fewer parts and less maintenance are required, and the premium pricing that many companies currently see in the aftermarket may evaporate. However, we do note that to this day Boeing and Airbus have not caught up on new airplane deliveries, and it is not the case that we will see any pandemic-like grounding of airplane fleets. The reductions will be measured and temporary, and that also means that airlines may actually elect to accelerate maintenance activities so they do not have to ground the airplanes now due to high oil prices and later on again when maintenance is due.

If we look at the sales by category and by end market, we note that 75% of the business is parts, 17% is MRO, and the remainder is specialty products. By end market, 60% is commercial aerospace, 30% is defense, and the remaining 10% is other industrial applications.

So, whereas commercial aerospace may see some slowing growth in demand, we also note that some safety-driven maintenance cannot be skipped, and some maintenance events will be accelerated for optimal scheduling. For HEICO, it would only be a huge negative if airlines start phasing out older airplanes that they kept in service to offset delayed new airplane deliveries, as those older airplanes require more parts and maintenance. If things get really bad, we will also see CapEx levers being pulled, in which case airlines may even elect to keep older airplanes in the fleet longer, providing even more support for the commercial business. In the remote event that OEM parts availability tightens, HEICO is positioned to provide PMA parts, which are certified parts. PMA stands for parts manufacturing approval and means that a PMA part is an FAA-certified part to replace parts produced by the original equipment manufacturer.

Furthermore, higher utilization of defense equipment feathers positively into the prospects for the part of the business that is driven by defense and government sales. The acquisition of Axillon Aerospace’s Fuel Containment business, which is a defense-focused company with exposure to the F-16, F-15, F/A-18, CH-47 Chinook, KC-135, B-52, AH-64 Apache, V-22 Osprey, and the Bradley Fighting Vehicle, is one of the recent acquisitions that could see upside to business from the current war deployment.

So, overall, there is definitely a risk for HEICO, but there are also some growth areas and rethinking of maintenance timing that may, in part, offset any reduction in flight-hour-driven maintenance events.

Analyst Estimates Have Barely Changed

While many aerospace and defense stocks have been under pressure as if the bottom will fall out any day now, analysts have barely revised their estimates. For HEICO, estimates did not even decrease. For FY2026 (fiscal year ending October 2026), estimates remained unchanged, while for the years ahead, there is a less than 1% positive adjustment to the estimates. It could, of course, be that analysts are wrong, but we note that the company has beaten estimates on EPS every year since 2014 and missed estimates four times over the same time frame. So, HEICO may in fact be more resilient than many investors think.

HEICO Stock Is Now Better Valued For A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that in the same way HEICO can purchase companies at a better price for its inorganic growth strategy, investors now have the opportunity to buy HEICO shares at a better price. We see that the stock price had gotten ahead of itself, trading at an EV/EBITDA of 38.4x for FY25, and that overvaluation is now gone, and in fact, against FY25 earnings, there is 5% upside, while current year expected earnings offer 20% upside with a base case price target of $326.13 and a bullish target of $366.62, offering 35% upside. Expected growth towards 2028 is 9.5% annually on sales, 10.5% on EBITDA, and 10.3% on free cash flow.

HEICO’s key metrics are looking good with project improvement in margins coupled with sales growth and strong cash conversion with growing free cash flow and low capital intensity. Absent any major M&A activity, the cash pile will continue to grow. In FY28, there would be some debt maturing, which would reduce the cash pile if HEICO does not refinance. In terms of net debt leverage, the situation is set to substantially improve, giving HEICO the possibility to either engage in M&A, for which I believe the current situation provides an opportune moment, or return value to shareholders.

Conclusion: HEICO Could Be One Of The Better Buys

I believe that there are indeed some challenges for HEICO, but only if the economy rapidly cools. Part of that is offset by airlines potentially scheduling maintenance earlier and increased demand for defense maintenance and parts. Overall, I do believe that the current share prices are much healthier today than they were before for shareholders to take positions. If there is one thing that we have seen several times, it is that HEICO and many other stocks have bounced back from global pressures that investors thought would be the end of things. So, I believe that this provides a very good opportunity to "Buy" HEICO at a more reasonable price.

data.title: Tesla: Three Scenarios, Zero Upside
data.description:
Setting The Stage

As I write this article (6th April, 2026), Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is commanding a market cap of about $1.35 trillion. This translates to a trailing P/E ratio of 335.12x and a forward multiple of about 174.57x on a consensus EPS of $2.06 by 2026.

In my view, this stock is not just expensive but also priced for a future version that is yet to materialize and might never appear. The present reality is one of a cascading series of delivery misses and deepening brand damages and intensifying competition.

In this article, I lay out a comprehensive multifactor bear thesis using a three scenario DCF framework anchored on realistic operational scenarios. My model factors the latest delivery data, brand research and competitive landscape.

Valuation: A Tower On Loose Soil

I will start with the numbers that give me and possibly any other investor a pause. The company’s trailing P/E of about 335x on a trailing EPS of just 1.08x sits substantially higher than its 5-year average of 188.24x. Looking at it against the top automakers average P/E of approximately 22.9x, it clearly shows that this company is overly overvalued.

To justify its current value of $360.59 per share, TSLA needs a flawless execution on every optionality embedded on its valuation. Be it the Full Self Driving[FSD] at scale, a profitable Robotaxi network, and a meaningful Optimus humanoid robot revenue all within a five year projection window. I used a three scenario DCF model as shown below to illustrate this empirically.

Model Assumptions

In my valuation, I assumed a discount rate of 9%, which is within range of the estimated WACC of Tesla by Value Investing.

This is a reflection of the company’s high beta of 1.94 and a business risk from margin compression and demand uncertainty as I will discuss in the succeeding sections. I used a terminal growth rate of 3% which matches the long-term nominal GDP growth which is appropriate for a maturing auto business.

The five year projection window gives TSLA sufficient runway to demonstrate FSD or Optimus monetization before terminal value kicks in. Revenue CAGRs of 4%, 12% and 22% across the bear, base and bull case are realistic. The bear case assumes modest brand recovery and margin stabilization and the bull case requires near perfect execution on autonomous and robotics. The terminal margins of 8%, 12% and 18% are similarly grounded. The 8% is a reflection of structurally commoditizing EV business, 12% approximating a recovered but competitive automaker and the 18% only achievable if high margin software and service revenue scale significantly. Lastly, I assumed the trailing outstanding shares of about 3.5 billion.

With these assumptions, all scenarios point to a meaningful downside from current levels ranging from -14% in the bull case to -76% in the bear case as presented above. This implies that the stock is overvalued under all modelled scenarios, and, therefore, at no given moment is the investor compensated for the risk he takes. As such, I believe this is an outright Sell.

A Deteriorating Operating Business

Narrative aside, what do the numbers say? For the FY 2025, TSLA posted a total revenue of $94.8 billion. This was its first-ever negative revenue growth and a continued decline in the YoY revenue growth rate.

This is being driven by price cuts, growing competition, and softer demand in key markets. The same trend is evident in the net income and operating margin.

In Q4 2025, vehicle deliveries declined by about 16% YoY to 418,227 units, and for the full year it dropped by approximately 9% to 1.64 million vehicles. In my view, this is not just a blip but a multi-quarter structural deterioration.

Additionally, automotive gross margins have fallen significantly from over 32% in early 2022 currently oscillating between 13% and 18% across 2024 and 2025. This marks a compression of nearly 20% driven by price cuts that clearly failed to stimulate volume meaningfully as shown by the drop in deliveries. Even worse, its ROE is just 4.93% and ROCE 4.20% in an apparent downtrend with a profit margin of 4.07%. The company trails its peer and main rival BYD (BYDDF), which is a clear testament of its weakening fundamentals. In my view, these are not the metrics of a company deserving a 300x P/E, instead they reflect an automaker with a legacy valuation problem.

A Look At The Brand: A Damaged Asset That’s Hard To Repair

Over the years, TSLA MOAT wasn’t just its technology, it was its brand. Unfortunately, this core strength has been eroded. Its brand value has fallen from about $43 billion at the start of 2025 to an estimated $27.6 billion by early 2026. This a down from a peak of $66.2 billion in January 2023. To add, Tesla’s recommendation score in the U.S. has reached a new low of 4/10, which clearly shows a brand losing its market appeal.

As if this is not enough, the company’s brand loyalty collapsed from 73% in June 2024 to 49.9% in 2025. Although it has partially recovered to 57.4%, the structural damage to Tesla’s core brand strength may be long lasting.

A Narrowing Competitive MOAT

I know the bulls argue that this company’s lead in software and battery technology is durable. However, the delivery data says otherwise. For instance, Chinese EV export surged 70% YoY in 2025 with much of that growth concentrated in the mass market segments where Tesla competes most directly. It even concerns me more that BYD has overtaken Tesla in global EV deliveries. It’s even expanding aggressively into Europe and South Asia with lower price points and improving product quality. This is a points to one obvious conclusion: that TSLA is surrendering its market leadership to BYD.

My Take On The Optionality Argument

I spoke to a bull the other day and his rejoinder to my bear take was that TSLA valuation is justified by the optionality. That is FSD, Robotaxi, Optimus and energy storage. This argument to be fair deserves engagement and not dismissal but also scrutiny.

Let me begin from the FSD side. Despite years of imminent full autonomy promise, Tesla’s technology remains classified as Level 2 driver assistance by regulators. Mind you, a commercial Robotaxi network requires level 4 or 5. Waymo, which has already deployed commercial robotaxi in multiple U.S. cities has a decade of lead time on geofenced operations. This tells us that FSD is not just far from a reality but also faces a stiff competition.

On the other hand, humanoid robotics at a commercial scale is said to be facing significant challenges that needs to be resolved before actualization. This makes its outlook blurred and therefore pricing that today in the more than 300x multiple in a company with declining margins to me doesn’t seem realistic.

Lastly, Tesla’s energy division has been a genuine bright spot, with strong Megapack growth representing a promising long-term driver.

However, with the segment contributing less than 15% of total revenue, even at the optimistic growth rates cannot close the valuation gap between the intrinsic value and the current price.

An Asymmetric Risk Reward Not Favoring Investors

Having walked you through the above sections, now consider what must go right for Tesla to merely justify its current price. FSD to achieve commercial level 4 autonomy, Optimus robots be manufactured at a meaningful scale before 2030, automotive margins recover to more than 18% despite intense competition and brand recovers sufficiently to recapture lost market share.

Look at this against what needs to go wrong for a significant further downside. Which is simply any one of the above bets failing to materialize. At a more than300x trailing P/E and a $1.35 trillion market cap, there is no margin of safety here. For a stock with a beta of 1.94 and a business model facing simultaneous brand damage, margin compression, delivery declines and intensifying competition, this risk profile is difficult to justify.

Conclusion

At the current price, one is potentially paying for what Tesla was envisioned to be with no discount for the very real possibility that it takes longer, costs more, and might not materialize at all in the worst case scenario. My DCF model clearly shows a downside risk of 14% to 76% from current levels with even the bull case offering negative upside potential. Consequently, I rate TSLA stock as a Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Shared 42 Sell Ratings In 2019. Here Is How They Have Performed Long-Term.
data.description:
Introduction

Estimating valuation is one of the most important skills an individual investor can learn. However, one of the biggest challenges with fundamental-based valuations is they aren't useful at all for predicting short-term stock price movements, especially those less than two years (which is unfortunate because that is the time-frame the vast majority of analysts and financial media focus on). Fundamental valuations' average correlation to stock prices takes many years to grow close enough to be useful. But when we get closer to a 10-year time frame, that is where stock prices correlate with valuations more tightly. Sometimes stocks can stay "expensive" for many years, making it seem as though valuations don't matter, but it's very rare for valuations to stay expensive for decades.

Fortunately, I have been writing about valuations on Seeking Alpha for over a decade now, so I have some long-term data to share about valuation outcomes. My early writings, from 2015-2018, mostly focused on investing in deep cyclical businesses, and, due to these businesses' very high earnings fluctuations, I used a different technique than a traditional earnings valuation for them. But starting in April 2019, I began sharing an earnings-based valuation technique I created called the "Full-Cycle Analysis," which projected out what future earnings (and stock price returns) were likely to be based on the previous economic cycle. During that time, because many stocks were expensive, I wrote a lot of "Sell" and "Strong Sell" articles. This article will examine the results of the 42 sell ratings I shared from April 2019 until the start of the pandemic in February 2020. So, we've had around 6-7 years for these valuations to mature, which will give us a clearer read on the usefulness of my techniques.

The Stocks Covered & Benchmarks

When I was writing bearish articles in 2019, I limited the universe of stocks I covered publicly to those that were in the S&P 500, and also to those that were of high enough quality I would consider buying them if the price was good enough. This is an important point. While writing these articles, I wasn't trying to find really bad businesses to short. I was specifically trying to warn investors about reasonably good businesses (sometimes even great businesses) whose valuations were high enough that they would be better off selling and just buying the index or a better-valued company. This is a very high bar when issuing sell ratings, and it is difficult to do well, but there is an added benefit that, for the most part, general business quality that isn't clearly expressed in the historical earnings of the companies, isn't likely to explain the outcomes. This leaves valuation as the most likely primary cause of the aggregate outcomes.

For those reasons, the benchmarks I will use for comparison in this article will be State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and also the equal-weighted Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) because the cap-weighted indexes are dominated by just 10 giant companies right now. These benchmarks should provide a good way to measure because nearly all of the sell-rated stocks were actual components of these indexes. Also, this will help account for the much higher inflation we've had since February 2020 compared to the 2009-2019 decade. Inflation has juiced nominal earnings growth and stock prices overall.

Seeking Alpha members can go to my profile page and still access all of these articles and data themselves, but I will provide a few screenshots with the ratings and dates for quick reference below.

Okay, hopefully, as you were scrolling through the list, you agree that these are high-quality businesses and popular stock holdings at the time. I didn't exclude any sell or strong sell ratings from the list, and I believe every stock was in the S&P 500 index (and most still are). Now lets examine the results. I used the total return price from Y-Charts from the publication date of the articles until the start of Q2 2026 to get the returns.

(BALL), Under Armour (UAA), Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), Waste Management (WM), Church & Dwight (CHD), Edwards Lifescience (EW), Apple (AAPL), Equifax (EFX), Proctor & Gamble (PG).

The average return of the sell ideas was +60% since publication, or, about a +7.50% nominal total return CARG. The average return of the cap-weighted S&P 500 (SPY) over the same time period was +139%, or, about a +14.34% CAGR. And the average return of the equal-weighted (RSP) was +97%, or, about a +10.99% CAGR.

Personally, given the dominance of a few megacap stocks in the SPY ETF, I think RSP is the better benchmark because it shows how this group of equal-weighted "sell" stocks" performed against the equal-weighted index from which they were selected. RSP significantly outperformed the performance of the bear stocks with a +97% return compared to +60%. It's also worth noting that inflation ran at a +3.71% annual rate during this time, so the sell stocks only returned a +3.79% real CAGR, and we are currently in a market that is near all-time highs. It's very possible that if there is a recession within the next couple of years (especially if inflation stays high), the real returns of the sell stocks could be close to zero by the time we get to year 10.

Of the 42 sell ratings, only eight outperformed RSP over the following 6-7 years. That's about an 81% success rate or 19% error rate at predicting underperformance. When I develop most of my strategies, I typically aim for about an 80% success rate, so these results are in line with what I would generally expect. The valuation method was successful both in terms of the magnitude of returns and in terms of the number of successes versus failures. This lets me know, especially with a good sample size of 42 well-diversified stocks pulled directly from the population of 500, that the valuation process is good at identifying overvalued stocks so they can be avoided (or sold if a person already owns them).

In short, there is a lot of evidence here that my valuation process is useful for investors and overall can help produce better returns by avoiding overvalued stocks. Next, I will share some of the fundamental principles of the approach so that readers can consider incorporating them into their own processes.

Principles of Valuation

I posted the results of these valuation calls first to let readers know that this isn't just philosophical musing. I used essentially the same process for each of the analyses, and it was repeated dozens of times. What comes next is actually the important part where I share some of the key principles I've adopted over the years that helped me develop my process. These principles can be used by anyone on a wide variety of stocks, but not every stock. Some businesses are not good fits for an earnings and earnings growth analysis. So, the first principle is:

Only apply the valuation technique to businesses that are good fits.

There are several criteria I require before I even perform a "Full-Cycle Analysis". The first is that we need enough historical earnings data. My preference is that we have public earnings data that includes at least one recession or a full business cycle. This has been trickier to do lately because the 2020 recession was so unusual and the last real recession was in 2009, but back in 2019, I required most stocks to have earnings data going back to 2006.

If the historical earnings were deeply cyclical (if earnings fell more than -50% during a downturn) or if they were just erratic and unpredictable, I didn't use an earnings-based valuation technique. Those stocks were excluded. (I do have a separate strategy for deeply cyclical businesses that doesn't use earnings.)

But if I had good long-term earnings histories and the earnings were relatively steady, then the businesses were good fits for the Full Cycle Analysis. Using a fundamental-based analysis for all stocks (at least doing so without an extraordinary amount of work and adjustments) will reduce the success rate of the technique. And, for what it's worth, I see investors and analysts do this all the time. For example, using a low P/E ratio for a cyclical business like Micron (MU) and determining it is "cheap", will lead to bad medium-term results most of the time because earnings can fluctuate a great deal.

History is the best guide to the future.

My experience implementing my core strategies (and the data has shown) that as long as a few additional factors are controlled for, long-term earnings history, properly calculated, is good at predicting the medium and long-term future of a stock about 80% of the time. Many, many, investors and analysts don't understand or ignore this fact. Even editors sometimes push back that my articles aren't forward-looking enough. And, indeed, I often don't spend much time predicting or analyzing upcoming quarterly earnings reports or the inner workings of the businesses or management. And sometimes those things do matter. It's just that 80%, or more, of the time, they are not as good at predicting the medium and long-term future of the stock as closely examining the history of the earnings, because ultimately earnings are the end result of all these other factors.

One of the main reasons for this is that most successful businesses are basically operating on slowly changing repeatable algorithms that provide products and services over and over again. If these systems have worked well over a full business cycle, they can likely keep doing what they have been doing successfully. The past, at least most of the time, is representative of the future when it comes to earnings.

Long-term, Stock Prices Follow Collectible Earnings

There are two metrics an investor needs to calculate or forecast well to estimate future returns: the starting earnings yield, and the long-term earnings growth rate, including downcycles. These two metrics put together do a very good job of estimating long-term returns (assuming most other things, especially debt, stay at similar levels). These metrics won't do a good job predicting short-term stock moves, but they work well in the 5-10 year range. They can also be good at predicting the potential downside risk from high valuation in the short-term, even though that risk may not come to fruition in a timely manner. In other words, an overvalued stock can fall at any time. It doesn't necessarily take five years, but as we get closer to the 10-year time frame, usually gravity will weigh on the majority of the overvalued stocks and cause them to underperform if the valuation work used reasonable estimates for the appropriate type of businesses.

Even the highest quality businesses can be overvalued

Stocks are a partial ownership of a business, and over the long-term the value of the business will be determined by the future earnings the business produces. No matter how good a business is, investors are capable of paying too much money for future earnings. The higher the price an investor pays for the business, the lower their share of future earnings will be, and the lower long-term returns will be. Eventually, the stock price will correlate with earnings and earnings growth. Business quality doesn't change that.

Side Notes On Mean Reversion

For most, the analyses back in 2019, when I first developed the Full-Cycle Analysis, I included a mean reversion expectation in the analysis. While that part of the analysis was based on historical market sentiment and can be quite useful, in more recent years, I have moved away from including mean reversion and instead focus entirely on business earnings and earnings growth. My reasoning for this is that we are no longer in a zero-interest-rate environment, and so I'm not sure we will go back to similar market multiples we had from 2008 to 2021. It's okay to make adjustments to the valuation process to take into account major macro-shifts.

Conclusion

Hopefully, readers found this data and valuation philosophy useful. I know it can be difficult to focus on the medium and long-term when almost everything and everyone is focused on short-term trading, or, quarterly earnings reports. But sound valuation techniques still matter, and will always matter, even if they need to be adjusted a bit around the edges over time. Revisiting these well-seasoned sell ratings and measuring their results, for what it's worth, has inspired me to write more valuation articles in the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
